topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop buzzed with the usual midday clamor, but my focus was entirely on Liam. He was laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound that used to be the soundtrack to my entire adolescence. I watched him, tracing the worn rim of my mug with a fingertip. He leaned back, gesturing expansively as he recounted some anecdote, completely oblivious to the subtle shift in the air around me. I tried to muster a smile, a practiced, easy curve of the lips, but it felt stiff, like a poorly fitted mask. When he finally turned to look at me, his eyes held a familiar warmth, but something in the way he looked through me—like I was a slightly out-of-focus photograph—made my stomach clench. "You look like you're solving a complex equation," he remarked, nudging my arm playfully. I just nodded, my gaze dropping to the condensation on the table. *It can't be real,* I thought, the conviction solidifying in my chest. *This is just a memory playing tricks on me.*

[story 2]

I pulled up the old shared photo album, the pages brittle and smelling faintly of dust and forgotten summers. There, there was Liam, grinning maniacally beside me on a poorly constructed sandcastle. I traced the outline of my younger self, the one who looked so utterly certain of our unbreakable bond. I flipped to the next page, searching for a shared inside joke, a mutual acknowledgment of the history we shared. Nothing. Just faded snapshots of group outings, and in every single one, Liam was present, vibrant, and utterly devoid of any recollection of the person standing beside him. A coldness seeped into my limbs, a sudden chill that had nothing to do with the air conditioning. I closed the book with a soft thud, my knuckles white against the cover. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to physically distance myself from the ghost of that friendship.

[story 3]

"Remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort in the woods?" I asked, trying to inject a casual lightness into my voice. Liam blinked, a slight furrow appearing between his brows as he searched his mind. He frowned, a genuine expression of confusion washing over his features. "A fort? I… I don't recall. Sounds like something I read about." He shrugged, a gesture that felt strangely dismissive, as if I were speaking a foreign language. I watched the confusion bloom on his face, and a strange, hollow sensation settled in my chest. I felt a tightening in my throat, a desperate urge to find some proof, some anchor to the shared reality I held so tightly. I forced my expression into something neutral, a bland acknowledgment, but the tremor in my hand as I reached for my water bottle betrayed the internal upheaval. *He doesn't know,* the thought echoed, sharp and undeniable.
